1: "The first day of the week" (tē mia tōn sabbatōn) is the day after the Jewish Sabbath — what we call Sunday. The Evangelist specifies "early" (prōi) and "while it was still dark" (skotias eti ousēs), which places Mary's arrival before dawn. The light/darkness motif has been central to the Fourth Gospel since the prologue (John 1:4–5, "the light shines in the darkness"); her arrival in the dark mirrors the Nicodemus episode (John 3:2) and anticipates a movement toward light. Women in Jewish society had primary responsibility for mourning and attending the dead; Mary's early visit to the tomb is consistent with this role.
2: Mary's use of "we do not know where they have put him" (plural oidamen) suggests she was not alone, as also implied by the Synoptic accounts (Mark 16:1; Luke 24:10 name several women). The Evangelist focuses on Mary as the central figure but preserves the plural verb, possibly reflecting an independent tradition or the author's knowledge of the fuller group. Her report to "the other disciple, the one Jesus loved" alongside Simon Peter pairs the two disciples who will race to the tomb — the Beloved Disciple being defined again by his relationship to Jesus rather than by his name.
3: The race to the tomb is one of the few passages in the Fourth Gospel that reads like unfiltered eyewitness memory — a specific physical detail (who ran faster, who arrived first, who entered first) that serves no obvious theological purpose and survives without symbolic elaboration. The Evangelist simply records it, which is itself significant: the care taken to distinguish what each disciple saw and did suggests the episode was told and retold in community memory in exactly these terms.
4: The other disciple outrunning Peter is noted without explanation or theological comment. This has been read as evidence of the Beloved Disciple's relative youth (physically faster) or as a social detail (he arrived first, which becomes relevant for the order of entry). Ancient readers would have read the foot-race detail with interest rather than puzzlement — competitive running was a recognized cultural activity, and the observation that one man outran another was perfectly ordinary.
5: The Greek parakupsas (bent over and looked in) is a physical posture of peering — the same verb is used in 1 Peter 1:12 for the angels longing to look into the gospel mysteries. Tomb entrances in first-century Judea were typically low openings in a rock face, requiring stooping or bending to see inside; the detail is architecturally accurate. The strips of linen lying there but the Beloved Disciple not entering is a carefully observed moment: he sees from outside the threshold, takes in the evidence, but pauses.
6: Peter's characteristic action — entering immediately without hesitation — is consistent with his portrayal throughout the Fourth Gospel (John 13:37; John 18:10; John 21:7) as one who acts before reflecting. The strips of linen (othonia) lying there are the same bandaging material used in the burial (John 19:40); the emptiness inside — cloths present, body absent — is what he sees. The arrangement of what remains becomes the physical puzzle that drives the next verses.
7: The careful specification of the burial cloth (soudarion) that had been around Jesus's head, lying not with the linen strips but "folded up by itself in a separate place," has been argued to distinguish the resurrection from a theft. Grave robbers working quickly would not have unwrapped the body and carefully folded the head cloth; the arrangement implies that the body was not moved but departed from its wrappings. The soudarion was a face-cloth or small linen piece used to bind the jaw during burial or to cover the face of the dead; its distinct placement is a specific detail that would have been immediately visible to Peter as he surveyed the tomb.
8: The Beloved Disciple enters after Peter, sees the same evidence, and "believed" — with no prior resurrection appearance and apparently no fuller understanding (as John 20:9 immediately concedes). What he believed is not specified: perhaps belief that the resurrection had occurred, triggered by the evidence that the body had not been taken but had departed differently. The Evangelist's sequencing gives the Beloved Disciple priority in faith (he believed first) while giving Peter priority in action (he entered first) — a characterological distinction that runs throughout the Gospel's treatment of these two figures.
9: The Evangelist's candid admission that the disciples did not yet understand "the Scripture that he must rise from the dead" is a significant piece of historical self-awareness. Resurrection expectation in Second Temple Judaism was widespread among Pharisees but typically referred to the general resurrection at the end of history, not an individual resurrection in the middle of history before the final judgment. No one in Jesus's circle had a framework for interpreting what they were seeing until the resurrection appearances themselves provided one; they had to learn retrospectively what the Scriptures had been pointing toward (cf. John 2:22; 12:16 for the same retrospective-understanding pattern).
10: The return home is narrated without joy or proclamation — the disciples process what they have seen but have not yet encountered the risen Jesus. The Evangelist places this anti-climactic moment deliberately: the empty tomb alone does not produce faith; it produces puzzlement. Mary, who stays behind weeping, will be the first to encounter the risen Christ — the sequence of the narrative emphasizes that the resurrection reveals itself through personal encounter, not just through physical evidence.
11: Jewish mourning (klaio, wailing/loud weeping) was a public, communal practice — not the private, internalized grief of modern Western culture. Professional mourners were hired for funerals (Matthew 9:23 mentions flute players and a noisy crowd); the mourner's role was to express grief loudly as an act of honor for the dead. Mary's weeping outside the tomb while stooping to look inside is an act of mourning combined with unwillingness to leave — the behavior of someone who cannot yet accept the situation as final. Her position (outside, bending in) mirrors the Beloved Disciple's physical posture at John 20:5, but her emotional state is opposite: he was detached enough to pause and observe; she is absorbed in grief.
12: The two angels in white seated where Jesus's body had been — one at the head, one at the feet — may be the Evangelist's deliberate evocation of the mercy seat (kapporet) in the Tabernacle, above which two golden cherubim faced each other with the space between them representing God's presence (Exodus 25:18–22). If so, the empty space where the body had lain corresponds to the space between the cherubim — the space that is now, paradoxically, the site of God's self-disclosure. Angels in white were standard equipment in Second Temple apocalyptic literature for moments of divine revelation (Dan 7; 1 Enoch; the Transfiguration accounts); their presence signals that what has happened is not a human event.
13: The angels' question "Woman, why are you crying?" is curious — why would angels ask this if they know the answer? In Jewish angelology and in the Gospels, heavenly messengers sometimes ask questions that function to draw out the human's state (cf. Genesis 3:9, God's "Where are you?" to Adam). Mary's response — "They have taken my Lord away and I don't know where they have put him" — frames the situation entirely within the category of grave desecration, the most serious transgression against the dead in Jewish law. Her use of "my Lord" (ton Kyrion mou) is an intimate possessive — not a theological title but a personal address — which makes the upcoming recognition scene all the more poignant.
14: The failure to recognize Jesus immediately upon turning around is a consistent feature of the resurrection appearances across all four Gospels (Luke 24:16, Emmaus; 21:4 here, and implicitly others). Various explanations have been proposed: grief and weeping impairing recognition, the resurrection body having altered characteristics, divine veiling for theological purposes, or simply the unexpectedness of the encounter (the person you saw die is the last person you expect to see walking in a garden). The Evangelist does not explain the non-recognition — he lets it stand as a feature of the encounter that the reader must process alongside Mary.
15: Thinking the stranger was "the gardener" (ho kēpouros) was reasonable in context: a garden required a caretaker, who would typically be a slave or employee responsible for maintenance and security. Mary's request — "if you have carried him away, tell me where you have put him and I will get him" — is both heartbreaking and impractical (a woman alone could not carry a body) but is driven by pure devotion rather than logistics. The garden setting activates resonances with Genesis: Adam was placed in a garden, humanity fell in a garden, and now the one who reverses the fall is encountered in a garden by one who mistakes him for the gardener.
16: The single utterance of her name — "Mary" — is the turning point of the encounter. The Good Shepherd passage (John 10:3–4) had described the Shepherd who "calls his own sheep by name" and whose sheep "know his voice"; here that description is enacted. Recognition comes through hearing, not through sight — an epistemological emphasis consistent with the Johannine pattern where belief comes through testimony and encounter rather than through visual proof alone. "Rabboni" is an emphatic Aramaic form of "Rabbi" — the doubled syllable is an affective intensification comparable to the difference between "teacher" and "my dear teacher." It is the word used for God in some Jewish prayers, though Mary uses it in its most personal register.
17: The prohibition mē mou haptou ("do not hold on to me") has generated extensive interpretation. Haptomai means not just "touch" but "cling to, attach oneself to" — the prohibition is not against physical contact per se (Jesus later invites Thomas to touch, John 20:27) but against Mary's apparent impulse to restore the previous relationship as if nothing had changed. The reason given — "for I have not yet ascended to the Father" — suggests that the resurrection is not the end of Jesus's movement but a transition toward ascension. The instruction "Go instead to my brothers and tell them" is a commission: Mary is sent as an apostle to the apostles. The reclassification of the disciples as "brothers" (adelphous) rather than "servants" (John 13:16) or "friends" (John 15:15) marks a new phase of relationship enabled by the glorification event.
18: Mary Magdalene's role as the first Easter witness and the first to announce the resurrection ("I have seen the Lord!") is historically remarkable given that women's testimony was legally inadmissible in both Roman courts and (per Josephus) in Jewish judicial proceedings. Josephus writes: "Let no woman be admitted as a witness, on account of the levity and boldness of their sex" (Ant. 4.8.15). If the early church were inventing the resurrection narrative, they would almost certainly not have chosen a woman as the primary witness. The inclusion of Mary Magdalene as the first witness is one of the details that historical critics regard as carrying a high probability of historical authenticity precisely because it is counterproductive to the narrative's reception.
19: The locked doors are emphasized ("the doors being shut," tōn thyrōn kekleismenōn) because they are the obstacle that Jesus's arrival defies. The disciples' fear of "the Jewish leaders" (hoi Ioudaioi) was grounded in the political reality that in Roman law a condemned criminal's associates could face charges related to the same offense; the disciples of an executed would-be king of the Jews were legally vulnerable. The peace greeting (Eirēnē hymin, "Peace be to you") is the standard Jewish greeting (shalom alechem), but in context it carries specific weight: the disciples were in fear, and the risen Lord's first word to them is peace — the peace he had promised in John 14:27 ("Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you").
20: The showing of the hands and side serves both to identify Jesus (these are the wounds from the crucifixion) and to demonstrate continuity between the crucified and the risen body — the resurrection did not erase the marks of the passion. The disciples' joy (echārēsan hoi mathētai, "the disciples rejoiced") fulfills the promise of John 16:20–22, where Jesus had told them their grief would turn to joy that no one could take from them. The fulfillment of the promise within the same book creates a narrative closure: the reader who remembers the Farewell Discourse is meant to recognize this moment as the promised reversal.
21: The mission statement "As the Father has sent me, I am sending you" parallels two different verbs in the Greek: apostellō (the Father "sent," from which "apostle" derives) and pempō (Jesus "sends") — the Evangelist uses these interchangeably for divine sending throughout the Gospel. The parallel structure is the foundation of the apostolic commission: the disciples' authority and mission are derived from, and shaped by, the way the Father sent the Son. Jesus received his mission from the Father with full authority; the disciples receive their mission from Jesus with derived authority. The mission framework presupposed by "as…so" implies similarity of pattern, not identity of status.
22: The act of breathing on them (enephusēsen) deliberately echoes the Septuagint of Genesis 2:7, where God "breathed into [Adam's] nostrils the breath of life" (enephusēsen eis to prosōpon autou pnoēn zōēs). The LXX uses the same rare compound verb (emphusaō) in both places — the Evangelist signals new creation by repeating the creative act of the first creation. This is the Fourth Gospel's account of Pentecost: what Acts 2 narrates as a corporate event with wind and fire, the Evangelist narrates as an intimate, private moment of direct breath. Whether the two accounts describe the same event (theologically) or different stages is a longstanding question; both present the gift of the Spirit as the transforming power behind the apostolic mission.
23: The authority to forgive or retain sins corresponds to the rabbinic and early Christian practice of community discipline. In Second Temple Judaism, the community at Qumran and the Pharisaic ḥavurot (fellowships) both exercised forms of exclusion and reinstatement that functioned as the retaining and releasing of sins within a defined community. The parallel in Matthew 16:19 and 18:18 uses the language of "binding and loosing" — technical terms in rabbinic law for declaring something permitted or forbidden, and for excommunicating or reinstating a community member. The early church understood this authority as applying to communal discipline and the liturgical pronouncement of absolution, not as granting individuals the power to determine cosmic destinies.
24: Thomas's absence from the first resurrection appearance places him in exactly the situation of subsequent generations: he has not seen; he has only heard the testimony of others who have. His subsequent demand for physical evidence ("unless I see the nail marks and put my finger where the nails were") is usually read as blameworthy skepticism, but it is worth noting that Thomas is asking for exactly the kind of evidence that Peter and the Beloved Disciple had just examined at the empty tomb — direct physical inspection. He is not refusing faith in principle; he is refusing to accept secondhand testimony as a substitute for firsthand evidence. The beatitude of John 20:29 ("blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed") is the Evangelist's response to this position, addressed to the reader.
25: Thomas's specification of "the nail marks" and "his side" identifies the specific wounds from the crucifixion as the evidence he requires. Roman crucifixion typically involved nails driven through the wrists (or hands/lower forearm, where the bone structure could support the body's weight) and through the feet; the spear wound to the side was confirmed in the Evangelist's account (John 19:34). Thomas's demand is for the same evidence the soldiers had already examined at the cross (John 19:32–34) — physical inspection of the crucified body. The fact that these wounds persist in the resurrection body is theologically significant: the resurrection affirms the crucifixion rather than erasing it.
26: "After eight days" (meth' hēmeras oktō) in the Jewish reckoning means a week later on the same day of the week — both the day of departure and the day of return are counted, so "eight days" from Sunday to the following Sunday. The pattern of Sunday appearances became the basis for the early Christian practice of assembling on the first day of the week (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2), distinguishing Christian worship from Jewish Sabbath observance. The second appearance follows the same pattern as the first (locked doors, "Peace be with you") but is specifically arranged for Thomas's benefit — the sequence of appearances is not random but responsive to the community's needs.
27: Jesus's invitation to Thomas — "Put your finger here; see my hands. Reach out your hand and put it into my side" — issues exactly the terms of evidence Thomas had demanded in John 20:25. Whether Thomas actually touches the wounds is left open by the Evangelist; the next verse jumps immediately to Thomas's confession without narrating physical contact. The invitation itself may have been sufficient: being addressed directly by the risen Jesus and invited to examine the wounds may have produced faith before the examination was completed. The key phrase "stop being unbelieving but believing" (mē ginou apistos alla pistos) is a present imperative with the implication of ongoing disposition — not merely a single act of belief but a settled orientation.
28: Thomas's confession "My Lord and my God!" (ho Kyrios mou kai ho Theos mou) is the climactic Christological statement of the Gospel, echoing and answering the prologue's "the Word was God" (John 1:1). The double title — Lord (Kyrios) and God (Theos) with the emphatic possessive mou (my) twice — is unparalleled in Jewish address to a human being. In Roman imperial culture, the title dominus et deus (lord and god) was used of the emperor Domitian (Suetonius, Domitian 13.2; Pliny, Pan. 2.3); if the Fourth Gospel was composed in the period of Domitian's reign (late first century), the confession reads as a counter-imperial declaration: Thomas applies to Jesus the title claimed by the emperor. The confession also recalls Psalms 35:23 (LXX John 34:23): "Awake and rise to my defense! Contend for me, my God and Lord" — a Psalm of the suffering righteous person vindicated by God.
29: The beatitude "Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed" addresses the situation of the Evangelist's community and of every subsequent reader. The Fourth Gospel was written after the generation of eyewitnesses had passed or was passing; the community it addresses could not see Jesus as Thomas did. The beatitude does not disparage sight-based faith (the whole Gospel is built on the testimony of witnesses who did see); it affirms that faith transmitted through testimony is sufficient, and those who believe on such testimony are not at a disadvantage compared to eyewitnesses. The Evangelist's stated purpose in John 20:30–31 confirms this: the book exists precisely so that people who were not present can come to faith.
30: The reference to "many other signs" not recorded in the Gospel (polla alla sēmeia) acknowledges that the text is a selection, not a comprehensive record. The word sēmeia (signs) has been the Fourth Gospel's primary category for Jesus's miracles throughout (the seven signs: water-to-wine, healing the official's son, healing the paralytic, feeding the five thousand, walking on water, healing the blind man, raising Lazarus). The selection from the available material is explicitly purposive — the Evangelist chose particular signs for a particular effect on a particular audience. Ancient readers would have recognized this as a standard authorial statement about the scope of a work.
31: The statement of purpose — "these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name" — is the Gospel's formal thesis statement, placed at its structural conclusion (before the appendix). The textual variant between pisteuēte (aorist, "come to believe") and pisteuēte (present, "continue believing") affects whether the Gospel is understood as primarily evangelistic (aimed at initial conversion) or catechetical (aimed at deepening existing faith); the manuscript evidence is divided, and both purposes likely coexist. The two titles — "the Messiah" and "the Son of God" — are the two halves of the Johannine confession: the Jewish messianic expectation (the anointed king from David's line) and the divine identity claim (the unique Son who shares the Father's nature). Both are required; neither alone is sufficient.
